# Questions in Livestream 107

Live Stream #107: A Hot Mess of Ebal Amulet Epigraphy

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction |
|---:|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=463" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">7:43</a> | Could both the `480 years` of 1 Kings 6:1 and the Ramesses reference be taken literally by dating the temple much later? | He said no; Judah-Assyria synchronisms lock Solomon's temple into the tenth century BC, so that workaround fails. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=592" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">9:52</a> | How did ancient Egyptian form the past tense? | He said Middle Egyptian typically marks it by inserting `n` into the verbal form before the suffix. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=1059" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:39</a> | Are there Egyptian monotheistic cult sites that parallel double altars or double standing stones? | He said yes; Amarna/Akhetaten gives examples of multiple altars, and such installations can have different ritual purposes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=1207" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:07</a> | Why was there still a redemption requirement for Israel's firstborn after the tenth plague? | He said the plague sparing and the later redemption rite serve different purposes, and `firstborn` is about heir status, not just newborn babies. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=1491" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:51</a> | Does using cultural context to understand scripture create relativism? | He said the opposite: cultural context helps clarify what the text meant instead of undermining it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=1887" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:27</a> | In Numbers, why did God require sacrifices when people were starving, and how did they survive? | He said most sacrifices were not as onerous as assumed, many fed families or Levites, and Deuteronomy frames obedience with promised provision. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=2308" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">38:28</a> | In Numbers 25:8, was Phinehas's stabbing symbolic or literal, and why were both people killed the same way? | He said the Hebrew depicts a literal, graphic act in which Phinehas pierced both offenders, stopping a plague tied to blatant communal sin. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=2725" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:25</a> | What are the holy tools in Numbers 31:6, and did Israel have holy war tools? | He said they were Tabernacle implements, not weapons, though captured enemy weapons could sometimes become symbols of God's deliverance. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=3022" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">50:22</a> | Was there a difference between murder and murder with a weapon, and why did the high priest need to die before returning? | He was unsure about the high-priest part, but said weapon language marks intent and means, while refuge cities handled accidental killings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=3496" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">58:16</a> | Is there a good Bible commentary covering almost every Old Testament verse, and what are good fantasy books or thoughts on The Witcher? | He said no single good commentary covers the whole Old Testament; use strong book-specific commentaries, and he mentioned Tolkien, Shadowrun, and limited experience with The Witcher. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=4002" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:06:42</a> | If the Father and Son are both God, why does Jesus seem to deny eye-for-eye and tooth-for-tooth rules? | He said Father and Son are distinct persons of the Trinity, and Jesus was not denying civil justice but adding mercy and grace to personal conduct. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=4266" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:11:06</a> | Is there a good book that reconciles the God of the Old Testament with the God of the New Testament? | He mainly warned that outright denial of the Old Testament is a Marcionite error and urged listeners toward readings that keep the full canon together. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=4603" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:16:43</a> | Did Gershon Galil's high-resolution image change the Mount Ebal tablet debate? | He said the improved image was welcome, but he still did not see the claimed inscription clearly enough to change his view. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=4944" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:22:24</a> | What is missing from history presented for a general audience, and what is scholarship often in denial about? | He said scholarship often lacks respect for texts, while popular history often misses cultural context, material culture, props, and costuming details. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=5414" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:30:14</a> | Could some Greek, Roman, Egyptian, or Indian gods be real, or could some be sons of God? | He said there may be demonic influence or legendary human figures behind some gods, but he was cautious about tying them directly to Genesis 6 sons of God. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=5632" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:33:52</a> | Did Hammurabi campaign into the Levant, and what do secular scholars think about Amraphel being Hammurabi? | He said Hammurabi reached Syria/the Levant, but whether he went as far south as Sodom and Gomorrah is uncertain; many scholars reject the Amraphel link on chronological grounds. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=5969" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:39:29</a> | Is it too far to use `church` in Matthew 16:18 as a public-space argument for Christians in politics? | He said yes for that passage, though Christians may serve publicly under kingdom ethics without forcing religious law or privileging Christians coercively. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=6207" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:43:27</a> | In Zechariah 9:9, what does the Hebrew word often rendered `salvation` mean? | He said the form is best understood as `victorious` in that context, because the verbal construction points to one able to help himself. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=6482" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:48:02</a> | Does the Song of Deborah indicate Yahweh was originally a Midianite war god? | He said the Midianite/Kenite hypothesis is plausible from Moses traditions, but the evidence is limited and the exact nature of Yahweh in Midian remains uncertain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=6800" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:53:20</a> | Why would King Abimelech be interested in marrying Sarah given her age? | He argued the patriarchal ages should not be read naively here, and the Sarah figure in the episode was likely much younger; a political angle is possible but not decisive. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=7236" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:00:36</a> | Were Shu, Tefnut, Geb, and other Egyptian gods thought to have human-shaped material bodies, or was that just iconography? | He said Egyptian gods could have both a natural substance and an anthropomorphic body, so the images were not merely decorative iconography. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=7499" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:04:59</a> | What should be said about Black Hebrew Israelites? | He described it as a recent African-American religious movement blending Afrocentrism and biblical claims, and said its historical arguments do not hold up. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=7801" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:10:01</a> | Why do Christians not believe in oral Torah? | He said Christians do not treat the Talmud or oral Torah as authoritative because much of it is late, speculative, and unreliable for original context, though it can still be read with value. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=8243" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:17:23</a> | From a biblical perspective, why did God allow other religions to develop, and why has he not intervened recently? | He said counterfeit religion implies a genuine one, and used the wheat-and-tares parable to argue that judgment is delayed to avoid harming those who may yet come to Christ. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=8563" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:22:43</a> | Are you more of an Old Testament or New Testament guy? | He said he is more drawn to the Old Testament because it is less worked over, spans more time and context, and remains essential for understanding the New Testament. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=8855" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:27:35</a> | What is your view of heaven, hell, and purgatory? | He rejected purgatory, affirmed heaven as eternal life and bliss with God, and deferred a fuller explanation of hell to a later show. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=9071" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:31:11</a> | Why did God choose Moses and the prophets, and what criteria did he use? | He said prophets are servants and intermediaries whose main work is often forth-telling the present clearly, and God uses fallible people for that task. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=9563" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:39:23</a> | If the Torah was edited during the exile to counter Marduk and Babylon, why prefer a fallen-priest interpretation over the apkallu? | He said he does not accept exile-era reshaping of the Torah in that way, favors earlier core documents with later stitching or updating, and sees the apkallu tradition as a poor fit. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=9948" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:45:48</a> | What resource or commentary on John would you recommend for a church class? | He recommended D. A. Carson's commentary on John. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=10444" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:54:04</a> | What about the claim that Jesus lied about prayer and faith because literal mountains do not move and believers do not receive everything asked in his name? | He said moving mountains is figurative, not a failed literal promise, and prayer also assumes God's will, goodness, and timing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=11005" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:03:25</a> | Does the wheat-and-tares answer also apply to where Jesus stops when quoting Isaiah 61:1-2 in Luke 4:17-21? | He said yes in broad pattern: Luke 4 leaves the day of vengeance for later, and the wheat-and-tares theme amplifies delayed judgment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=11306" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:08:26</a> | Who was the earliest known Egyptian king, and what do we know about him? | He said Narmer or Hor-Aha are candidates for the earliest unified king, while the earliest recorded royal name fragment on the Palermo Stone may read `Poo`. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=11617" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:13:37</a> | Do you recommend K. A. Kitchen's `On the Reliability of the Old Testament`? | He said yes; it is dated and needs a second edition, but remains valuable and has not really been replaced. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=12004" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:20:04</a> | How are ancient sites identified, such as Troy or Jericho? | He said identifications combine geography, texts, archaeology, continuity of local names, inscriptions, archives, and informed inference, with Egyptian sites often easier when labels survive. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=12590" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:29:50</a> | Is the Ipuwer Papyrus good evidence for the Exodus plagues? | He said it is not direct evidence because it is a Middle Kingdom text, but later copying clusters may suggest Egyptians used old prophetic literature to process crisis memories. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/DClj9BDRQLQ?t=13049" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:37:29</a> | How would you critique the idea that all religions are rivers flowing into the same ocean and lead to the same God? | He rejected it because religions make contradictory claims about God's attributes, and he appealed to Christ as the exclusive way to the Father. |
